description_long: This is a weathered, rectangular fuel dispenser with a metallic body, showing significant rust and grime across its surfaces. The front panel features two digital display windows, labeled for 'Diesel' and 'Unleaded' fuel types, with faded red and green text above each. Below the displays, there are two fuel nozzles, each attached to a long black hose, which are holstered on the sides of the dispenser. The left side of the unit has a visible vented panel and a holstered fuel nozzle, while the right side mirrors this with another nozzle and vent. A vertical strip of safety and warning labels is affixed to the lower left front, adding color and detail. The overall design is utilitarian, with no decorative elements, and the unit appears to be constructed primarily from metal with plastic and rubber components.
description: A rusted, rectangular metal fuel dispenser with digital displays for diesel and unleaded, two black hoses with nozzles, and safety labels on the front.
description_view_0: Front view showing digital displays for diesel and unleaded, safety labels, and the main control panel.
description_view_1: Left side view with a vented panel and a holstered black fuel nozzle and hose.
description_view_2: Rear view showing a plain, rusted metal surface with no visible features or controls.
description_view_3: Right side view with a vented panel and a holstered black fuel nozzle and hose.
attribute_fuel_pumps: Two visible fuel pumps integrated into the dispenser, each with a black hose and nozzle holstered on the left and right sides. The style is utilitarian and somewhat vintage, with digital display panels above each pump. No visible branding on the pumps.
attribute_station_signage: Signage includes 'DIESEL' in red and 'UNLEADED' in green above the digital displays on the front panel, and a vertical strip of safety and warning labels in multiple colors on the lower left front. No main brand or large directional signs are visible.
attribute_price_display_board: No price display board is visible.
